examples.doc: Gets value from input `dict` by the name of attribute defined in template as constant value.
examples.name: AttrSimpleFixedName

examples.doc: Attempts to get a value from input `dict` by the name that does not exist.
examples.name: AttrSimpleNameDoesNotExist

examples.doc:
Tries to get a value from nested input structure of `dicts` with paths defined in different attribute of input data.
Actual path is not correct at its second component.
Template results with no value.
examples.name: AttrSimplePathDoesNotExist1

examples.doc:
Tries to get a value from nested input structure of `lists` with paths defined in different attribute of input data.
Actual path is not correct at its second component.
Template results with no value.
examples.name: AttrSimplePathDoesNotExist2

examples.doc: Gets value from input `list` by the index defined in template as constant value.
examples.name: AttrSimpleFixedIndex

examples.doc: Attempts to get a value from input `list` by the index that does not exist.
examples.name: AttrSimpleIndexDoesNotExist

examples.doc:
When a dynamic attribute name evaluates to no value, lookup returns no value
uniformly — including on lists, where an invalid index type would otherwise fail.
examples.name: AttrDynamicNameNoContent

examples.doc: Gets value from input `dict` by the name of attribute defined in different attribute of input data.
examples.name: AttrDynamicReferenceName1

examples.doc: Gets value from input `dict` by the name of attribute defined in different attribute of input data.
examples.name: AttrDynamicReferenceName2

examples.doc: Gets a `list` of values with 4 elements discovered in nested input structure of `dicts` with paths defined in template.
examples.name: AttrSimpleFixedNames

examples.doc: Gets value from nested input structure of `dicts` with paths defined in different attribute of input data.
examples.name: AttrDynamicReferenceNames

examples.doc:
Gets a `list` of values discovered in nested input structure of `dicts` with paths defined in input data.
List of paths may include any number of paths of any depth.
examples.name: AttrDynamicReferenceMultipleNames

examples.doc: Searching for values in nested structure with sequence of `attr` operations chained together.
examples.name: ChainWithAttr

examples.doc:
Evaluates each arm's `when` in order and emits the `then` of the first truthy
one. Only the selected `then` is walked; later arms are skipped. This is the
ordered, multi-way generalization of an `if`/`else` chain.
examples.name: CondFirstTruthyArm

examples.doc: Falls back to the `default` template when no arm's `when` is truthy.
examples.name: CondDefaultBranch

examples.doc: Converts input string into integer in base-16.
examples.name: ConvertValues

examples.doc:
Adds 100 to input integer and converts result into string.
Illustrates usage of `call` attribute `value`.
examples.name: ConvertValue

examples.doc: Converts input integer into string.
examples.name: ConvertNoParameters

examples.doc: Parses an input string into a floating-point number using the `float` function.
examples.name: ConvertToFloat

examples.doc: Returns a fallback string when a missing attribute is looked up.
examples.name: AttrWithDefault

examples.doc: Returns a fallback number when an undefined variable is requested.
examples.name: GetWithDefault

examples.doc: Returns a fallback label when a formatted value is missing.
examples.name: FormatWithDefault

examples.doc:
Adds suffix to input string.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule in form of monad.
examples.name: ExprSimpleMonads1

examples.doc:
Adds prefix to input string.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule in form of monad and usage of `parent` rule.
examples.name: ExprSimpleMonads2

examples.doc:
For input dictionary containing two integers returns sum of squares.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule in form of monad.
examples.name: ExprMonadsComplex

examples.doc:
Adds suffix to input string.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule with direct parameters.
examples.name: ExprSimpleValues1

examples.doc:
Adds prefix to input string.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule with direct parameters.
examples.name: ExprSimpleValues2

examples.doc:
For input dictionary containing two integers returns sum of squares.
Illustrates functionality of `expr` rule with direct parameters.
examples.name: ExprValuesComplex

examples.doc:
With `values`, the operator runs as `reduce(op, evaluated_list)` — the current
context value is not an implicit argument. Input data affects the result only
when you reference it explicitly inside a list item (e.g. `{"$": "this"}`).
examples.name: ExprValuesIgnoresThis

examples.doc:
`and`/`&&` use Python logical conjunction (truthiness), not bitwise `&`.
With integers, `6 and 3` yields `3`, not `2`.
examples.name: ExprLogicalAnd

examples.doc:
`or`/`||` use Python logical disjunction (truthiness), not bitwise `|`.
Returns the first truthy operand or the last operand.
examples.name: ExprLogicalOr

examples.doc: `or` treats `NO_CONTENT` as falsy, so a missing value falls through to the fallback.
examples.name: ExprOrNoContentFallback

examples.doc: Calculates negation of input using `expr` rule with unary operator as monad.
examples.name: ExprUnary1

examples.doc: Calculates negation of input using `expr` rule with unary operator in alternative form.
examples.name: ExprUnary2

examples.doc:
The ``file`` rule writes output through a ``file_writer`` delegate passed to
``Transformer(...)``. It always returns ``NO_CONTENT`` (shown as ``null`` in JSON),
so ``map`` over it yields an empty list — the side effect is the write, not the
transform result. ``name`` and ``content`` are templates; if either evaluates to
``NO_CONTENT``, nothing is written for that invocation.
examples.name: FileWriteViaMap

examples.doc: Iterates over input `dict` and filters out items with even value.
examples.name: FilterDict

examples.doc: Iterates over input `list` and filters out items with even value.
examples.name: FilterList

examples.doc:
Filters a list, keeping only elements at odd indices (1, 3, 5, ...).
Demonstrates that ``index`` works inside ``filter`` as well as ``map``.
examples.name: FilterListByOddIndex

examples.doc:
Sets some names to context, one with `true` value and one with `false`.
Iterates over input `list` of string and filters out items with name that is not set to `true` in the context.
examples.name: FilterListNoContent

examples.doc: Iterates list with floats and produces list of strings by formatting each item.
examples.name: FormatFromSingleValue

examples.doc: Iterates over list of dicts and produces list of string using items for filling pattern slots.
examples.name: FormatFromDict

examples.doc: Iterates over list of lists and produces list of string using items for filling pattern slots.
examples.name: FormatFromList

examples.doc:
Iterates over list of values.
For formatting value creates dict with dynamically calculated values.
Produces list of strings using formatting.
examples.name: FormatWithValue

examples.doc: Formats a dict using a pattern string taken from the same input.
examples.name: FormatWithDynamicPattern

examples.doc:
Produces no result when a formatted value is missing instead of interpolating
the sentinel repr into the output string.
examples.name: FormatSkipsMissingValue

examples.doc: Returns the length of a string with `length`.
examples.name: LengthOfString

examples.doc: Returns the length of an array with `length`.
examples.name: LengthOfArray

examples.doc: Flattens one level of an array-of-arrays with `flatten`.
examples.name: FlattenOneLevel

examples.doc: Sums an array of numbers with `sum` (`sum([])` is `0`).
examples.name: SumNumbers

examples.doc: Returns the minimum element of an array with `min`.
examples.name: MinOfArray

examples.doc: Returns the provided default when `max` is called on an empty array.
examples.name: MaxWithDefault

examples.doc: Returns a new ascending-sorted array of homogeneous scalars with `sorted`.
examples.name: SortedArray

examples.doc: Deduplicates an array while preserving first-occurrence order with `unique`.
examples.name: UniquePreserveOrder

examples.doc: Returns the absolute value of a number with `abs`.
examples.name: AbsNumber

examples.doc: Returns the floor of a number with `floor`.
examples.name: FloorNumber

examples.doc: Returns the ceiling of a number with `ceil`.
examples.name: CeilNumber

examples.doc: Rounds a number to a given number of digits with `round`.
examples.name: RoundWithDigits

examples.doc: Converts a value to a boolean by Python truthiness with `bool` (total).
examples.name: BoolOfNull

examples.doc: Converts epoch seconds to a fixed ISO-8601 UTC timestamp with `from_epoch`.
examples.name: FromEpochIso

examples.doc: Formats epoch seconds with an explicit whitelist format via `from_epoch`.
examples.name: FromEpochCustomFormat

examples.doc: Parses a fixed ISO-8601 UTC timestamp to epoch seconds with `to_epoch`.
examples.name: ToEpochIso

examples.doc: Parses a custom-format UTC date string to epoch seconds with `to_epoch`.
examples.name: ToEpochCustomFormat

examples.doc: Encodes a UTF-8 string as standard-alphabet base64 with `b64encode`.
examples.name: B64Encode

examples.doc: Decodes standard-alphabet base64 to a UTF-8 string with `b64decode`.
examples.name: B64Decode

examples.doc: Builds a deterministic UUID5 from a well-known namespace and a name.
examples.name: Uuid5Dns

examples.doc: Returns capture groups from `regex_match` on a successful match.
examples.name: RegexMatchGroups

examples.doc: Returns `null` from `regex_match` when the pattern does not match.
examples.name: RegexMatchNone

examples.doc: Replaces regex matches with `regex_replace`.
examples.name: RegexReplace

examples.doc: Uppercases a string with the `upper` function.
examples.name: UpperString

examples.doc: Lowercases a string with the `lower` function.
examples.name: LowerString

examples.doc: Capitalizes a string with the `capitalize` function.
examples.name: CapitalizeString

examples.doc: Replaces all occurrences of a substring with `replace`.
examples.name: ReplaceSubstring

examples.doc: Removes an exact prefix with `removeprefix` (unlike character-set `lstrip`).
examples.name: RemovePrefix

examples.doc: Removes an exact suffix with `removesuffix`.
examples.name: RemoveSuffix

examples.doc: Strips leading and trailing whitespace with `strip`.
examples.name: StripWhitespace

examples.doc: Strips a leading character set with `lstrip`.
examples.name: LstripChars

examples.doc: Strips a trailing character set with `rstrip`.
examples.name: RstripChars

examples.doc: Slices a string with `slice` using start and stop indices.
examples.name: SliceString

examples.doc: Slices an array with `slice` from a start index to the end.
examples.name: SliceArray

examples.doc: Reverses a string with `reversed`.
examples.name: ReversedString

examples.doc: Reverses an array with `reversed`.
examples.name: ReversedArray

examples.doc:
Returns no result when a named attribute is absent from the input.
Used as an included sub-template in ``IncludeWithDefault``.
examples.name: LookupMissingAttr

examples.doc: Returns a fallback when the included template produces no result.
examples.name: IncludeWithDefault

examples.doc:
Illustrates usage of `include` rule by iterating over input `list` and applying different template to each item.
Produces a `list` of results applying `MapListsToDict` template.
For all examples in this documentation you can use them under their name in include command.
This was achieved by providing special `template_loader` factory to `Transformer` class.
examples.name: Include

examples.doc: Enriches input dict with default value.
examples.name: JoinWithStaticNoOverrides

examples.doc: Enriches input dict with default value.
examples.name: JoinWithStaticWithOverrides

examples.doc: Joins two dicts taken from separate attributes of input into one dict.
examples.name: JoinTwoDictsNoCommonKeys

examples.doc: Joins two dicts taken from separate attributes of input into one dict.
examples.name: JoinTwoDictsWithCommonKeys

examples.doc: Joins two dicts taken from separate attributes of input into one dict.
examples.name: JoinTwoStrings

examples.doc: Joins two strings using a separator taken from input data.
examples.name: JoinTwoStringsWithDynamicSep

examples.doc:
Iterates over input list twice.
First time produces list of `dicts` with keys and values defined in separate attributes of input items.
Second time produces list of `dicts` but with keys and values swapped.
Joins together results of two iteration into single list.
Then joins together items of the list into single resulting object.
examples.name: JoinManyDynamicDicts

examples.doc: Joins optional string fields, omitting any that are missing.
examples.name: JoinSkipsMissingStringFields

examples.doc: Joining an empty list of items produces no value (`NO_CONTENT`).
examples.name: JoinEmptyReturnsNoContent

examples.doc: When there are no items to join, `default` supplies the result value.
examples.name: JoinEmptyWithDefaultDict

examples.doc:
Maps over a list input, wrapping each element in a one-item list around an object
with an ``x`` field. Matches the headline intro example: the outer ``test`` key is
copied literally, the nested ``map`` rule runs once per input element, and the inner
``item`` rule resolves to each element's value.
examples.name: MapListToNestedListWithItem

examples.doc:
Maps two lists obtained from different attributes of input.
Then joins two obtained lists into single resulting list.
examples.name: MapListToList

examples.doc: Iterates over input dict and produces list of dicts with corresponding fields for keys and values.
examples.name: MapDictToList

examples.doc: Iterates over input dictionary and produces dictionary with keys and values swapped.
examples.name: MapDictToDictItems

examples.doc: Iterates over input dictionary and produces interlaced list of both keys and values.
examples.name: MapDictToListItems

examples.doc:
Zips two lists containing keys and values together into pairs.
Then iterates over list of pairs and produces resulting dict.
examples.name: MapListsToDict

examples.doc:
Gets variable names `foo` that does not exist from context.
Then tries to get attribute named `bar` of non-existing value.
This results into no value which translates into `None`.
examples.name: NoContentForAttr

examples.doc:
Creates a dict with item key that has no value.
It results with empty object.
examples.name: NoContentForObjectKey

examples.doc:
Creates a dict with item value that has no value.
It results with empty object.
examples.name: NoContentForObjectValue

examples.doc:
Builds a multi-key object in `fields` mode where one value has no value.
The entry whose value resolves to no value is omitted from the result.
examples.name: NoContentForObjectFields

examples.doc: Demonstrates skipping of items in `list` generation by `map` rule when they have no value.
examples.name: NoContentForMappingList

examples.doc: Demonstrates skipping of items in `dict` generation by `map` rule when they have no value.
examples.name: NoContentForMappingDict

examples.doc:
Builds a single-item dict whose attribute name comes from the input.
Both the key and the value are dynamic templates.
examples.name: ObjectDynamicKeyValue

examples.doc:
Produces an object whose key is the marker (`$`) itself.

In `fields` mode the keys of the mapping are emitted verbatim, so `$` is kept
as literal data instead of being interpreted as a rule invocation. The values
are still walked as templates, so the emitted document can mix literal marker
keys with dynamic values — here transon is used to generate a transon template.
examples.name: ObjectFieldsLiteralMarker

examples.doc:
Builds a multi-key object with literal keys and dynamic values.

This is handy for documents that use `$`-prefixed operator keys (e.g. MongoDB
queries): the keys stay literal while each value is interpolated from input.
examples.name: ObjectFieldsMultipleLiteralKeys

examples.doc:
`<`/`lt` returns `true` when the current context value is strictly less than the
given value.
examples.name: OperatorLessThan

examples.doc:
`<=`/`le` returns `true` when the current context value is less than or equal to the
given value.
examples.name: OperatorLessOrEqual

examples.doc: `==`/`eq` returns `true` when both operands are equal.
examples.name: OperatorEqual

examples.doc: `!=`/`ne` returns `true` when the operands differ.
examples.name: OperatorNotEqual

examples.doc:
`>=`/`ge` returns `true` when the current context value is greater than or equal to
the given value.
examples.name: OperatorGreaterOrEqual

examples.doc:
`>`/`gt` returns `true` when the current context value is strictly greater than the
given value.
examples.name: OperatorGreaterThan

examples.doc: `-`/`sub` subtracts the given value from the current context value.
examples.name: OperatorSubtract

examples.doc: `*`/`mul` multiplies the current context value by the given value.
examples.name: OperatorMultiply

examples.doc: `/`/`div` performs true division; the result is a float.
examples.name: OperatorDivide

examples.doc:
`%`/`mod` returns the remainder of dividing the current context value by the given
value.
examples.name: OperatorModulo

examples.doc:
`in` returns `true` when the left operand is an element of an array.
Total — never raises.
examples.name: OperatorInArray

examples.doc: `in` returns `true` when the left operand is a key of an object.
examples.name: OperatorInObjectKey

examples.doc: `in` returns `true` when the left operand is a substring of a string.
examples.name: OperatorInString

examples.doc:
**Read a deeply nested value.**

*Task: "I have a value buried under several keys — how do I pull it out?"*

Give `attr` a `names` path instead of a single `name` and it walks the keys in
order, returning the value at the end (or *no value* if any segment is missing).
examples.name: RecipeReadNestedValue

examples.doc:
**Fall back to a default when a field is missing.**

*Task: "How do I substitute a value when the input doesn't have the key?"*

`attr` returns *no value* for a missing key; add a `default` template and that
value is used instead. The same `default` parameter exists on `get`, `format`,
`join`, and `include`.
examples.name: RecipeDefaultForMissingField

examples.doc:
**Pull one field out of every item (project a list).**

*Task: "How do I turn a list of records into a list of just one of their
fields?"*

`map` in `item` mode rebuilds each element; here the new element is just the
record's `email`, read by `attr`. The other fields are dropped.
examples.name: RecipePluckFieldFromEach

examples.doc:
**Swap the keys and values of a dict.**

*Task: "How do I invert a mapping so the values become the keys?"*

Iterating a dict, `map` exposes each entry through the `key` and `value`
accessor rules. Building the result in `key`/`value` mode with them crossed over
inverts the mapping.
examples.name: RecipeSwapKeysAndValues

examples.doc:
**Join a list of strings into one string.**

*Task: "How do I concatenate a list into a single delimited string?"*

`join` concatenates its `items`; when they are all strings it inserts the `sep`
between them. Here `this` feeds the whole input list straight in.
examples.name: RecipeJoinListToString

examples.doc:
**Build a string from a record's fields.**

*Task: "How do I interpolate fields into a template string?"*

`format` runs Python's `str.format`; when the value is a dict it is unpacked, so
the pattern can reference fields by name (`{first}`, `{last}`).
examples.name: RecipeBuildStringFromFields

examples.doc:
**Convert a value to a different type.**

*Task: "My number arrived as a string — how do I parse it?"*

`call` invokes a registered conversion function (`str`, `int`, `float`) on its
`value`; here `int` parses the `"42"` read by `attr` into the number `42`.
examples.name: RecipeConvertType

examples.doc:
**Map a code to a display label.**

*Task: "My input has a short status code — how do I turn it into a
human-readable string?"*

`switch` compares its evaluated `key` against the literal keys of `cases` and
returns the matching template's value; only the selected case is evaluated.
An unknown code falls through to `default`.
examples.name: RecipeMapCodeToLabel

examples.doc:
**Bucket a value by ranges.**

*Task: "How do I classify a number into small/medium/large?"*

`cond` evaluates its arms in order and selects the first whose `when` is
truthy. Each `when` here is an `expr` comparison against the piped value
(`chain` puts the number into the current context first); values matching
no arm fall through to `default`.
examples.name: RecipeBucketValueByRanges

examples.doc:
**Compute a value once, use it twice.**

*Task: "How do I avoid repeating the same calculation in two output fields?"*

`set` stores the current context value under a variable name (and passes it
through unchanged), so a `chain` can compute a derived value, store it, and
let later steps read it back with `get` as many times as needed.
examples.name: RecipeComputeOnceUseTwice

examples.doc:
**Pair up two parallel lists into records.**

*Task: "I have matching lists of names and scores — how do I merge them into
a list of objects?"*

`zip` transposes the two lists into `[name, score]` pairs; `map` then rebuilds
each pair as an `object`, with `attr` reading list items by numeric index.
examples.name: RecipePairUpTwoLists

examples.doc:
**Keep only the items matching a condition.**

*Task: "How do I filter a list on a comparison, not just a truthy flag?"*

`filter` keeps the items whose `cond` is truthy; the condition here is an
`expr` comparison (`price >= 100`) built from the item's own fields, so any
predicate expressible with operators works — not only boolean flags.
examples.name: RecipeKeepItemsMatchingCondition

examples.doc:
A variable stored at one literal-dict key is visible to later sibling keys
evaluated in the same scope (dict insertion order).
examples.name: SetVisibleToLaterSiblingKey

examples.doc:
A variable stored at one literal-dict key is not visible to sibling keys
that were already evaluated — visibility depends on dict key order.
examples.name: SetInvisibleToEarlierSiblingKey

examples.doc:
The first func of a `chain` runs in the caller's context, so a `set` there
is visible to later sibling templates outside the `chain`.
examples.name: SetInFirstChainFuncVisibleToSibling

examples.doc:
A `set` in a non-first `chain` func writes to a derived context and is not
visible to sibling templates outside the `chain`.
examples.name: SetInLaterChainFuncNotVisibleToSibling

examples.doc:
A `set` in the first `chain` func is copied into derived contexts and is
visible to later funcs in the same `chain`.
examples.name: SetInFirstChainFuncVisibleToLaterFunc

examples.doc:
A variable stored before a `map` is copied into each iteration context and
is visible inside the loop.
examples.name: SetBeforeMapVisibleInsideIteration

examples.doc:
A variable stored inside a `map` iteration is not visible after the loop
ends — each iteration uses a derived context.
examples.name: SetInsideMapNotVisibleAfterLoop

examples.doc: Splits a string on a separator into a list of strings — the inverse of `join`.
examples.name: SplitString

examples.doc: Splits an array into sub-arrays on elements equal to `sep`.
examples.name: SplitArrayOnElement

examples.doc: Splits an array on each contiguous occurrence of an array `sep` (subsequence).
examples.name: SplitArrayOnSubsequence

examples.doc:
Dispatches on a value read from the input and emits the matching case. Only the
selected case template is evaluated — the other arms are never walked — so
`switch` is the lazy alternative to building a dict and indexing it.
examples.name: SwitchDispatchOnKey

examples.doc:
Falls back to the `default` template when no case matches the dispatch key
(this also covers the case where the key evaluates to no value).
examples.name: SwitchDefaultBranch

examples.doc: Reports the JSON type of an object with the `type` function.
examples.name: TypeOfObject

examples.doc: Reports the JSON type of an array with the `type` function.
examples.name: TypeOfArray

examples.doc: Reports the JSON type of a string with the `type` function.
examples.name: TypeOfString

examples.doc: Reports the JSON type of an integer with the `type` function.
examples.name: TypeOfInt

examples.doc: Reports the JSON type of a floating-point number with the `type` function.
examples.name: TypeOfFloat

examples.doc:
Reports the JSON type of a boolean with the `type` function (note booleans are
distinguished from integers).
examples.name: TypeOfBoolean

examples.doc: Reports the JSON type of `null` with the `type` function.
examples.name: TypeOfNull

examples.doc:
Dispatches on a node's JSON type with a non-throwing `switch` whose key is the
`type` function — the canonical pattern a generated codec uses to walk an
arbitrary document.
examples.name: TypeDispatchWithSwitch

examples.doc:
**Arithmetic via nested rules — no string DSL.**

transon has no expression mini-language: you compose arithmetic by *nesting*
`expr` rules, each one a plain JSON object. Here `(a + b) * c` is built by feeding
the result of an inner `+` into an outer `*`, with every operand read from the
input by `attr`. Where JSONata or jq would write a terse `(a + b) * c`, transon
keeps the expression as data — so it can be stored, generated, diffed, and
validated like any other JSON.
examples.name: WorkedExampleNestedArithmetic

examples.doc:
**Reshape a list of records (line items → billing summary).**

The everyday JSON-transformation task — the same shape JSONata demonstrates with
`Account.Order.Product.(Price * Quantity)` and jq with
`.items | map({sku, amount: (.qty * .price)})`. `chain` pulls the `items` list out
of the input with `attr`, then `map` rebuilds each record as a new `object` whose
`amount` field is computed on the fly by an `expr` multiplication. Five rules —
`chain` → `attr` → `map` → `object` → `expr` — compose into one declarative
template, showing how the primitives documented separately below combine into a
real transformation.
examples.name: WorkedExampleReshapeRecords

examples.doc:
**Rename keys and flatten a nested record.**

The signature task of structural mappers like Jolt's `shift` spec: take a nested
document and emit a flat one with renamed keys. `object` in `fields` mode lists the
output keys literally; each value pulls from the input — `attr` with `names`
walks a path (`profile → firstName`) to flatten, and `format` joins first/last into
a single `fullName` (the equivalent of JSONata's `firstName & " " & lastName`).
examples.name: WorkedExampleRenameAndFlatten

examples.doc:
**Filter a list, then project a field.**

The classic select-and-pluck pipeline: JSONata writes `users[active].name`, jq
writes `map(select(.active)) | map(.name)`. transon composes it from two rules in a
`chain` — `filter` keeps the records whose `active` flag is truthy, then `map`
projects each survivor down to its `name`, dropping the other fields.
examples.name: WorkedExampleFilterAndProject

examples.doc:
**Index a list into a lookup dict (key-by).**

Turning a list of records into a dict keyed by one of their fields — jq's
`INDEX(.code)`, lodash's `keyBy` — is a staple of data shaping. `map` in its
`key`/`value` mode builds the dict directly: `attr` picks the `code` for each
key and the `name` for each value, collapsing the list (and discarding the other
fields) into an O(1) lookup table.
examples.name: WorkedExampleIndexByKey

examples.doc:
**Optional fields and defaults (the `NO_CONTENT` model).**

Conditionally including a field is awkward in many tools (JSON-e reaches for
`$if`, Jolt for cardinality tricks). In transon it falls out of the data model:
a value that resolves to *no value* is simply skipped. Here `phone` is absent from
the input, so `attr` yields `NO_CONTENT` and `object` omits the key entirely;
meanwhile `tier` is missing too but supplies a `default`, so it lands as `"free"`.
One uniform rule — skip *no value*, fall back on `default` — covers both
"drop when absent" and "substitute when absent".
examples.name: WorkedExampleOptionalFieldsAndDefaults

examples.doc:
**Conditional enrichment inside a map (per-record branch logic).**

*The shape most real templates take*: iterate a list of records and rebuild
each one with fields that depend on the record's own values. `map` drives the
iteration and `object` shapes each output record; inside it, `switch`
dispatches a short plan code to its display label (with a `default` for
unknown codes), while `cond` buckets the numeric `seats` field with an `expr`
comparison. Only the selected branch of each dispatch is ever evaluated —
where JSONata would write nested ternaries and jq an `if/elif` chain, transon
keeps every branch a plain JSON template.
examples.name: WorkedExampleConditionalEnrichmentInsideMap

examples.doc:
Transposes parallel lists into rows of paired values.
Each row is a list (not a Python tuple), so the result is JSON-friendly.
examples.name: ZipLists

examples.doc: Strings are iterables: zipping them pairs characters, like Python's `zip`.
examples.name: ZipStrings

functions.doc: Convert any value to its string representation (Python `str`).
functions.name: str

functions.doc: Parse a string (or number) into an integer (Python `int`). An optional base can be passed as a second value.
functions.name: int

functions.doc: Parse a string (or number) into a floating-point number (Python `float`).
functions.name: float

functions.doc: Return the JSON type of a value: one of `object`, `array`, `string`, `int`, `float`, `boolean`, or `null`. Total — never raises on well-formed JSON, so it is the one operation a `switch`/`cond` key can safely apply to an unknown node.
functions.name: type

functions.doc: Convert any value to a boolean by Python truthiness. Total — never raises. Useful to turn `regex_match` (groups-or-null) into a condition predicate.
functions.name: bool

functions.doc: Return `s` uppercased. Non-string input raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: upper

functions.doc: Return `s` lowercased. Non-string input raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: lower

functions.doc: Return `s` with the first character uppercased and the rest lowercased. Non-string input raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: capitalize

functions.doc: `values: [s, old, new]` — replace all occurrences of `old` with `new` in `s`.
functions.name: replace

functions.doc: `values: [s, prefix]` — remove `prefix` from the start of `s` if present. Unlike `lstrip`, this is exact-prefix removal, not character-set trimming.
functions.name: removeprefix

functions.doc: `values: [s, suffix]` — remove `suffix` from the end of `s` if present. Unlike `rstrip`, this is exact-suffix removal, not character-set trimming.
functions.name: removesuffix

functions.doc: Strip leading/trailing whitespace, or `values: [s, chars]` for a character set. Not a substitute for `removeprefix`/`removesuffix`.
functions.name: strip

functions.doc: Strip leading whitespace, or `values: [s, chars]` for a character set.
functions.name: lstrip

functions.doc: Strip trailing whitespace, or `values: [s, chars]` for a character set.
functions.name: rstrip

functions.doc: `values: [x, start]` or `[x, start, stop]` — Python slice of a string or array. Negative indices and out-of-range clamps apply. Non-int indices raise `TransformationError`.
functions.name: slice

functions.doc: Convert epoch **seconds** (int/float; fractional truncated) to a UTC string. Unary form uses fixed ISO-8601 `YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssZ`. `values: [n, fmt]` uses an explicit format restricted to `%Y %m %d %H %M %S %j %z %%` plus literal text. Non-finite or out-of-range inputs and disallowed directives raise `TransformationError`.
functions.name: from_epoch

functions.doc: Parse a UTC date/time string to epoch seconds (int). `values: [s]` accepts fixed ISO-8601 `YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssZ`; `values: [s, fmt]` uses an explicit format with the same whitelist as `from_epoch`. Parse failure raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: to_epoch

functions.doc: Return the length of a string, array, or object. Other types raise `TransformationError`.
functions.name: length

functions.doc: Flatten one level of an array-of-arrays. Non-array input or a non-array element raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: flatten

functions.doc: Sum an array of numbers. `sum([])` is `0`. Booleans and non-numeric elements raise `TransformationError`.
functions.name: sum

functions.doc: `values: [array]` or `[array, default]` — minimum element. Empty array without `default` raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: min

functions.doc: `values: [array]` or `[array, default]` — maximum element. Empty array without `default` raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: max

functions.doc: Return a new array of homogeneous scalars sorted ascending. Mixed types raise `TransformationError`.
functions.name: sorted

functions.doc: Reverse an array or a string. Other types raise `TransformationError`.
functions.name: reversed

functions.doc: Deduplicate an array preserving first-occurrence order. Object or array elements raise `TransformationError`.
functions.name: unique

functions.doc: Absolute value of a number. Non-number raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: abs

functions.doc: Floor of a number (`math.floor`). Non-number raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: floor

functions.doc: Ceiling of a number (`math.ceil`). Non-number raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: ceil

functions.doc: Round a number; optional `values: [x, ndigits]`. Non-number raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: round

functions.doc: UTF-8 encode a string and return standard-alphabet base64.
functions.name: b64encode

functions.doc: Decode standard-alphabet base64 to a UTF-8 string. Invalid base64 or non-UTF-8 payload raises `TransformationError`.
functions.name: b64decode

functions.doc: `values: [namespace, name]` — deterministic UUID5. `namespace` is one of `dns`/`url`/`oid`/`x500` or a UUID string. Pure and reproducible (no random `uuid4`).
functions.name: uuid5

functions.doc: `values: [s, pattern]` — Python `re.search`. On match returns capture groups as an array (unmatched optionals are `null`); with no groups returns `[full match]`. On no match returns `null`. Use `bool(...)` in conditions. Invalid pattern raises `TransformationError`. Dialect is Python `re`; ReDoS is a host responsibility.
functions.name: regex_match

functions.doc: `values: [s, pattern, repl]` — Python `re.sub`. Invalid pattern raises `TransformationError`. Dialect is Python `re`; ReDoS is a host responsibility.
functions.name: regex_replace

operators.doc: Less-than comparison: `true` when the left operand is strictly less than the right.
Equality comparison: `true` when both operands are equal.
Inequality comparison: `true` when the operands differ.
Addition for numbers, concatenation for strings.
True division; the result is a float.
Modulo: the remainder of a division.
Logical conjunction by truthiness (Python `and`). Returns one of the operands, not a strict boolean.
Logical disjunction by truthiness (Python `or`). Returns the first truthy operand and treats `NO_CONTENT` as falsy.

Membership: `true` when the left operand is a member of the right. Array → element membership; string → substring (left must be a string, else `false`); object → key presence (left must be a string, else `false`); any other container → `false`. Total — never raises.
operators.name: in

rules.description:
Returns the value stored in current transformation context.
The root context has the value equals to input of transformation.
Each operation creates new context with value of its result.
rules.name: this

rules.description:
Returns the value stored in previous context.
Using `parent` in the root context (where no previous scope exists) raises
`DefinitionError`.
rules.name: parent

rules.description:
Works inside `map`/`filter` when iterating over lists.
Returns current item.
Using it outside such an iteration scope raises `DefinitionError`.
rules.name: item

rules.description:
Works inside `map`/`filter` when iterating over dicts.
Returns the key of current element.
Using it outside such an iteration scope raises `DefinitionError`.
rules.name: key

rules.description:
Works inside `map`/`filter`.
Returns 0-based index of iteration.
Using it outside an iteration scope raises `DefinitionError`.
rules.name: index

rules.description:
Works inside `map`/`filter` when iterating over dicts.
Returns the value of current element.
Using it outside such an iteration scope raises `DefinitionError`.
rules.name: value

rules.description:
Stores `context.this` under the given name in the *current* context scope.
Returns `context.this` unchanged (pass-through), so it can sit inside a
`chain` without altering the piped value.

Visibility follows the language's downward-only scoping model (Language
Reference, "Context and scoping"): the variable is visible to scopes derived
*after* the `set` and to later-evaluated siblings sharing the same scope —
never to parent scopes once a derived scope ends, and never across an
`include` boundary (a separate transformation). Note the refactoring
pitfall: wrapping a step in `chain`, reordering dict keys, or moving a `set`
can change visibility with no error.
rules.name: set

params.description: Name of the variable. Can be dynamic. The names `this`, `item`, `key`, `value` and `index` are reserved and cannot be used as variable names. Raises `TransformationError` if the name evaluates to `NO_CONTENT`. See the rule docstring for scoping rules.
params.name: name

rules.description:
Returns the value stored under the given name in the *current* context.

Ancestor variables are available because `derive()` links child scopes to their
parent and `get` walks that chain — but only variables that were already `set`
before that derivation. A `set` in a sibling template or a later `chain` func that
shares no context object with this evaluation will not be found.

Returns `NO_CONTENT` when the name is undefined, unless `default` is
provided.
rules.name: get

params.description: Name of the variable. Can be dynamic. The names `this`, `item`, `key`, `value` and `index` are reserved and cannot be used as variable names. Raises `TransformationError` if the name evaluates to `NO_CONTENT`. See the rule docstring for scoping rules.
params.name: name

params.description: Optional template for value returned when the variable is undefined. Can be dynamic.
params.name: default

rules.description:
Returns values of attribute or item from current value in context.
Can search in deeply nested structures with path.

- A missing key or an index out of range → no value (`NO_CONTENT`), or the
  `default` when provided. Looking further into a missing value stays
  missing — a deep path over an absent branch does not raise.
- If the dynamic name or any path segment evaluates to `NO_CONTENT` → no
  value (uniformly for all container types).
- Any other lookup failure (e.g. indexing a string with a string) raises
  `TransformationError`.

Parameters are mutually exclusive.
rules.name: attr

params.description:
Name of attribute to search. 
It is possible to use a number as the name for items in arrays. 
Can be dynamic.
params.name: name

params.description:
List of attribute names or indexes to search in nested structure. 
Can be dynamic.
params.name: names

params.description: Optional template for value returned when the attribute or path is missing. Can be dynamic.
params.name: default

rules.description:
Returns a dict.

In the default `key`/`value` mode it returns a dict with a single,
dynamically named item; this is useful when the attribute name itself comes
from the input. If you need multiple attributes use `join` or `map`. If `key`
or `value` returns no result then the rule returns an empty dict.

In the `fields` mode it builds a dict from a literal mapping: each key is
emitted verbatim and each value is walked as a template. Because the keys are
literal, this is the canonical way to produce an object whose key is the
marker (`$`) itself — data that would otherwise be interpreted as a rule
invocation. Values that evaluate to `NO_CONTENT` are skipped, omitting the
corresponding key from the result.
rules.name: object

params.description: Defines template for name of attribute. Mutually exclusive with `fields`.
params.name: key

params.description: Defines template for value of attribute. Mutually exclusive with `fields`.
params.name: value

params.description: A literal mapping whose keys are emitted verbatim — including the marker (`$`), so this is how you produce an object key that is the marker itself (data that would otherwise be read as a rule invocation). Each value is walked as a template. Entries whose value evaluates to `NO_CONTENT` are omitted. Mutually exclusive with `key`/`value`.
params.name: fields

rules.description:
Iterates over `list` or `dict` and produces new `dict` or `list` with items based on template.

Each iteration derives a sub-context whose `this` is the element, with the
iteration accessors available (`item`/`index` over lists; `key`/`value`/`index`
over dicts). Modes (mutually exclusive):

- `item`: one output element per input element → list.
- `items`: the template yields a *list* of elements per input element; the
  lists are concatenated → list (the flatten-while-mapping mode).
- `key`+`value`: one output entry per input element → dict.

Results that evaluate to `NO_CONTENT` are skipped — the item (or the whole
key/value pair) is omitted from the output rather than emitted as `null`.
Iterating a value that is neither a list nor a dict raises
`TransformationError`.
rules.name: map

params.description: Defines template for items when producing the `list`
params.name: item

params.description: Defines templates for series of items when producing the `list`
params.name: items

params.description: Defines template for key when producing the `dict`
params.name: key

params.description: Defines template for value when producing the `dict`
params.name: value

rules.description:
Iterates over `list` or `dict` and filters out items regarding condition calculation.

Keeps the elements whose `cond` evaluates truthy; a condition that evaluates
to `NO_CONTENT` excludes the element. The container type is preserved:
list → list, dict → dict (entries keep their original keys and values).
Filtering a value that is neither a list nor a dict raises
`TransformationError`.
rules.name: filter

params.description: Defines template for condition
params.name: cond

rules.description:
Transposes iterables like Python's `zip`: rows become columns and columns become rows.
Each output row is a **list** (JSON-friendly), not a Python tuple.
The result is as long as the shortest input. A non-iterable item raises
`TransformationError`.
rules.name: zip

params.description: Defines the list of lists
params.name: items

rules.description:
Writes a file using `write_file` delegate (a parameter to `Transformer` constructor).
This rule always produces no result (`NO_CONTENT`) — so a `map` over `file`
yields `[]`, and a template that only writes files transforms to `None`.
File will not be written if `name` or `content` returns no result.
rules.name: file

params.description: Defines template for file name
params.name: name

params.description: Defines template for file content. File content always has JSON formatted data
params.name: content

rules.name: join

params.description: List of values to concatenate.
params.name: items

params.description: Defines separator for strings concatenation. Can be dynamic. Used only when all items are strings; must evaluate to a string (raises `TransformationError` otherwise). Defaults to empty string.
params.name: sep

params.description: Optional template for value returned when there are no items to join (including when all items are `NO_CONTENT`). Can be dynamic.
params.name: default

rules.description:
Splits a string or an array on a separator — the inverse of string/list `join`.

- String input → list of strings. `sep` must be a non-empty string.
- Array input → list of lists. `sep` is either a single non-array element
  (split where elements equal `sep`) or a non-empty array (split on each
  contiguous occurrence of that subsequence). Because an array `sep` means
  subsequence, you cannot split on a separator *element* that is itself an
  array.
- `NO_CONTENT` input → `NO_CONTENT`.
- Any other input type → `TransformationError`.
rules.name: split

params.description: Separator used to split the current context value. Can be dynamic. For a string input must evaluate to a non-empty string. For an array input may be a single non-array element (split on `==`) or a non-empty array (split on each contiguous subsequence). Empty string/array `sep` raises `TransformationError`.
params.name: sep

rules.description:
Function composition operation.
Build function composition and executes it against value in current context.
`chain(f1, f2, f3)(x) === f3(f2(f1(x)))`

Each function execution creates new context that will be passed to next function.
The **first** func runs in the caller's context (so a `set` there is visible
outside the `chain`); each subsequent func runs in a derived context.
```plantuml
@startuml
left to right direction
rectangle this
rectangle "intermediate context" as i1
rectangle "intermediate context" as i2
rectangle result
this --> i1: f1
i1 --> i2: f2
i2 --> result: f3
@enduml
```
rules.name: chain

params.description: Define list of templates (not just rules) for chaining. The list structure is constant; each element is walked as a template.
params.name: funcs

rules.description:
Executes unary or binary operation.
There are 3 modes of running this rule:

1. **No attributes were specified:**
Result is unary operation calculation against current context value.
2. **Single `value` was specified:**
Result is binary operation calculation where the
first argument is current values of context and
the second is specified value.
3. **Multiple `values` were specified:**
Result is calculated by applying operation to all values in pairs (reduction).
In this case current context value is ignored.
`values` must be a non-empty list — an empty reduction has no seed and
raises `DefinitionError`.

Parameters are mutually exclusive. An operator applied to incompatible
operand types raises `TransformationError`. The application semantics shared
by all operators (modes, reduction, type coercion, `NO_CONTENT` interaction)
are specified in the Language Reference ("Expressions and calls").
rules.name: expr

params.description:
Defines name of operation. This is always constant.

###### Possible values:

| operator     | alternative | kind   | types          | result         |
|--------------|-------------|--------|----------------|----------------|
| <            | lt          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| <=           | le          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| ==           | eq          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| !=           | ne          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| >=           | ge          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| >         | gt          | binary | any            | boolean        |
| +            | add         | binary | string, number | string, number |
| -            | sub         | binary | number         | number         |
| *            | mul         | binary | number         | number         |
| /            | div         | binary | number         | number         |
| %            | mod         | binary | number         | number         |
| &&           | and         | binary | any            | any            |
| || | or          | binary | any            | any            |
| !            | not         | unary  | boolean        | boolean        |
| in           | in          | binary | any            | boolean        |

You can use code-style or mnemonic name (i.e. operator or alternative).
params.name: op

params.description: Defines template for single parameter value
params.name: value

params.description:
Defines template for multiple parameter values.

> **Warning:** Unlike unary mode and `value` mode, **`this` is not used** when `values`
> is specified. The operator is applied via `reduce(op, values)` over the evaluated
> list only. Include `{"$": "this"}` as a list item if you need the current context
> value in the reduction.
params.name: values

rules.description:
Runs function registered in `Transformer`.
Usually, that is used to convert values into different type, but you can also use it to call any function.

There are 3 modes of running this rule:

1. **No attributes were specified:**
Runs conversion function with single parameter - value of current context.
2. **Single `value` was specified:**
Runs conversion function with single parameter - provided value.
Current context is ignored in this case.
3. **Multiple `values` were specified:**
Runs conversion function with multiple parameters (`values` must be a list;
a non-list raises `DefinitionError`).
Current context is ignored in this case.

Parameters are mutually exclusive. Built-in functions convert their
documented failure modes into `TransformationError`. The application
semantics shared by all functions (modes, argument passing, totality,
`NO_CONTENT` interaction) are specified in the Language Reference
("Expressions and calls").
rules.name: call

params.description:
Defines the name of function. This is always constant.

###### Possible values:

| name          | input                | output         |
|---------------|:--------------------:|:--------------:|
| str           | any                  | str            |
| int           | str                  | int            |
| float         | str                  | float          |
| type          | any                  | string         |
| bool          | any                  | boolean        |
| upper         | string               | string         |
| lower         | string               | string         |
| capitalize    | string               | string         |
| replace       | string               | string         |
| removeprefix  | string               | string         |
| removesuffix  | string               | string         |
| strip         | string               | string         |
| lstrip        | string               | string         |
| rstrip        | string               | string         |
| slice         | string, array        | string, array  |
| from_epoch    | number               | string         |
| to_epoch      | string               | int            |
| length        | string, array, object| int            |
| flatten       | array                | array          |
| sum           | array                | number         |
| min           | array                | any            |
| max           | array                | any            |
| sorted        | array                | array          |
| reversed      | string, array        | string, array  |
| unique        | array                | array          |
| abs           | number               | number         |
| floor         | number               | int            |
| ceil          | number               | int            |
| round         | number               | number         |
| b64encode     | string               | string         |
| b64decode     | string               | string         |
| uuid5         | string               | string         |
| regex_match   | string               | array, null    |
| regex_replace | string               | string         |
params.name: name

params.description: Defines template for single parameter value
params.name: value

params.description: Defines template for multiple parameters values
params.name: values

rules.description:
Execute string formatting using current value.

If `value` is specified it is used for formatting otherwise current context value is used.

See [this page](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/inputoutput.html#the-string-format-method)
for examples of how formatting in python works.

If value for formatting is `dict` it will be unpacked to separate names.
If value is list if will be unpacked to separate items.
Otherwise while value will be passed to formatting.

Returns no value when the formatting value (or any unpacked list element or dict
key/value) is `NO_CONTENT`, unless `default` is provided.
A pattern referencing a key or index the value does not supply raises
`TransformationError`.
rules.name: format

params.description: Defines pattern for string formatting. Can be dynamic. Must evaluate to a string (raises `TransformationError` otherwise).
params.name: pattern

params.description: Defines template for input data. Optional.
params.name: value

params.description: Optional template for value returned when the formatting value (or any unpacked list element or dict key/value) is `NO_CONTENT`. Can be dynamic.
params.name: default

rules.description:
Loads and runs another template using the `template_loader` delegate
(a parameter to `Transformer` constructor).

Only the current context value is passed to the included template — variables
stored with `set`/`get` and iteration accessors do not cross the boundary.

The `template_loader` is always called as `loader(name, context=...)`, where
`context` is an `IncludeContext` carrying the parent's loader, marker, depth
guard, and include-stack. The loader is responsible for **constructing** the
sub-`Transformer` with them (e.g. via `context.transformer(template)`), which
inherits the parent's marker by default — so a sub-template using the default
marker stays consistent across the boundary, recursive / self-`include`ing
templates re-resolve without each host re-patching the loader, and the loaded
instance is never mutated. Pass an explicit `marker` to `context.transformer`
to pin a different marker for the sub-template.

Nested includes are limited by `max_include_depth` on `Transformer` (default 50).
Exceeding the limit raises `TransformationError` with the include name chain.

If the included template produces no result, this rule produces no result unless
`default` is provided.
rules.name: include

params.description: Name or path to template. Can be dynamic. Meaning of this `name` depends on provided template loader.
params.name: name

params.description: Optional template for value returned when the included template produces no result. Can be dynamic.
params.name: default

rules.description:
Lazy equality dispatch: evaluates `key`, then evaluates **only** the matching
entry of `cases`. Non-selected case templates are never walked, so `switch`
differs from building a dict and indexing it (which would evaluate every arm).

Matching is by equality between the evaluated `key` and the literal case keys.
When no case matches — including when `key` evaluates to `NO_CONTENT` — the
`default` template is evaluated if present, otherwise the rule produces no
value.
rules.name: switch

params.description: Template for the value to dispatch on. Can be dynamic. Compared by equality against the (literal) keys of `cases`.
params.name: key

params.description: A mapping from case value to result template. Only the template of the selected case is evaluated; the others are never walked.
params.name: cases

params.description: Optional template evaluated when no case matches (including when `key` evaluates to `NO_CONTENT`). Can be dynamic.
params.name: default

rules.description:
Lisp-style ordered multi-way conditional (subsumes a chained `if`/`else`).
Walks each arm's `when` in order; the **first** truthy `when` selects its
`then`, which is the only `then` evaluated. A `when` that evaluates to
`NO_CONTENT` is treated as falsy. When no arm matches, the `default` template
is evaluated if present, otherwise the rule produces no value.
rules.name: cond

arms.description: Predicate template for this arm. Can be dynamic. The first arm whose `when` is truthy is selected.
arms.name: when

arms.description: Result template, evaluated only when this arm is selected. Can be dynamic.
arms.name: then

params.description: Ordered list of `{when, then}` arms. The first arm whose `when` is truthy selects its `then`; later arms and non-selected `then` templates are never walked.
params.name: cases

params.description: Optional template evaluated when no arm matches. Can be dynamic.
params.name: default
